disease.name: Esophageal Cancer
disease.note: Progressive dysphagia and weight loss are cardinal features. Squamous cell carcinoma (mid-esophagus, linked to smoking/alcohol) and adenocarcinoma (distal, linked to Barrett's/reflux) are the main histologic types. Late presentation is common in East Africa — many patients present with complete obstruction or tracheoesophageal fistula.
presentingComplaints: Difficulty swallowing food starting with solids then liquids
Food getting stuck in the chest
pathophysiologyDetail: p53 tumor suppressor gene mutation (TP53) occurs in >70% of esophageal squamous cell carcinoma (ESCC) and >80% of adenocarcinoma (EAC) — mutations are early events in carcinogenesis
p16/CDKN2A inactivation via homozygous deletion, promoter methylation, or mutation in 50-70% of EAC disrupts Rb-mediated cell cycle arrest at G1/S checkpoint
Cyclin D1 (CCND1) amplification and overexpression in 30-50% of ESCC drives cell cycle progression and correlates with lymph node metastasis and poor prognosis
EGF receptor (EGFR/HER1/ErbB1) overexpression in 30-80% of ESCC activates PI3K/AKT/mTOR and RAS/RAF/MEK/ERK pathways promoting proliferation and invasion
HER2/ErbB2 amplification in 15-30% of EAC (similar to gastric cancer) — target for trastuzumab-based therapy in HER2-positive advanced disease
VEGF-A overexpression drives tumor angiogenesis via VEGFR-2 signaling, promoting microvascular density and hematogenous metastasis; anti-angiogenic targets (ramucirumab) are clinically validated
miRNA dysregulation: miR-21 (oncomiR) upregulation suppresses PTEN and PDCD4; miR-375 downregulation correlates with poor differentiation; miR-200 family loss promotes EMT via ZEB1/ZEB2

Field cancerization: multifocal genetic alterations throughout the esophageal epithelium from chronic carcinogen exposure (alcohol, tobacco, nitrosamines) — explains synchronous and metachronous lesions
Barrett's metaplasia → low-grade dysplasia → high-grade dysplasia → EAC sequence driven by bile acid and acid reflux activating NF-κB, CDX2, and MUC2 expression
Telomerase reactivation via hTERT promoter mutations (20-40% of EAC) enables immortalization and is an early event in Barrett's carcinogenesis
Smokeless tobacco and nitrosamine-rich foods (smoked fish, roasted meat, traditional brews) contribute to high ESCC incidence in East Africa via O6-alkylguanine DNA adduct formation
Wnt/β-catenin pathway activation in 30% of EAC via CTNNB1 mutations or APC loss leads to nuclear β-catenin accumulation and MYC/CCND1 transcription
Epigenetic silencing of tumor suppressors (DAPK1, MGMT, APC, RARB2) via promoter CpG island hypermethylation is an early and frequent event in both ESCC and EAC
NOTCH1 mutations found in 10-20% of ESCC (often truncating, tumor suppressor role) contrasts with activating NOTCH1 mutations in other cancers
Tumor microenvironment: cancer-associated fibroblasts (CAFs) secrete matrix metalloproteinases (MMP-2, MMP-9), TGF-β, and HGF promoting invasion and immune evasion

Programmed death-ligand 1 (PD-L1) expression on tumor cells and tumor-infiltrating lymphocytes correlates with immune evasion; checkpoint inhibitors (nivolumab, pembrolizumab) are increasingly used in second-line and now first-line settings

landmarkTrials.trial: CROSS Trial (Chemoradiotherapy for Oesophageal Cancer Followed by Surgery Study)
landmarkTrials.population: 366 patients with resectable esophageal cancer (squamous and adenocarcinoma), randomized to neoadjuvant chemoradiation (carboplatin + paclitaxel + 41.4 Gy) followed by surgery vs surgery alone
landmarkTrials.finding: Median overall survival 49.4 months (neoadjuvant) vs 24.0 months (surgery alone). 5-year OS 47% vs 33%. Complete pathological response in 29% of patients (49% in squamous cell, 23% in adenocarcinoma). R0 resection rate 92% vs 69%.
landmarkTrials.impact: Established neoadjuvant chemoradiation with carboplatin/paclitaxel as standard of care for resectable esophageal and GEJ cancer. The CROSS regimen is now the most widely used neoadjuvant protocol worldwide.
landmarkTrials.reference: van Hagen P, Hulshof MC, van Lanschot JJ, et al. Preoperative chemoradiotherapy for esophageal or junctional cancer. N Engl J Med. 2012;366(22):2074-2084.

landmarkTrials.trial: MAGIC Trial (Medical Research Council Adjuvant Gastric Infusional Chemotherapy)
landmarkTrials.population: 503 patients with resectable gastric, GEJ, or lower esophageal adenocarcinoma randomized to perioperative chemotherapy (epirubicin + cisplatin + 5-FU ×3 cycles pre and post) vs surgery alone
landmarkTrials.finding: 5-year overall survival 36% (perioperative chemo) vs 23% (surgery alone). Progression-free survival significantly improved. Tumor size and nodal status both significantly better in chemotherapy group.
landmarkTrials.impact: Established perioperative chemotherapy as a standard for resectable GEJ and gastric adenocarcinoma. The MAGIC regimen was standard for >15 years before being superseded by FLOT.
landmarkTrials.reference: Cunningham D, Allum WH, Stenning SP, et al. Perioperative chemotherapy versus surgery alone for resectable gastroesophageal cancer. N Engl J Med. 2006;355(1):11-20.

landmarkTrials.trial: FLOT-4 / AIO Trial (FLOT vs ECF/ECX Perioperative Chemotherapy)
landmarkTrials.population: 716 patients with resectable gastric or GEJ adenocarcinoma randomized to FLOT (docetaxel + oxaliplatin + 5-FU + leucovorin) vs ECF/ECX (epirubicin + cisplatin + 5-FU/capecitabine)
landmarkTrials.finding: Median OS 50 months (FLOT) vs 35 months (ECF). 3-year OS 57% vs 48%. Pathological complete response 16% vs 6%. FLOT was superior across all endpoints.
landmarkTrials.impact: FLOT replaced ECF/ECX as the preferred perioperative chemotherapy regimen for resectable gastric and GEJ adenocarcinoma worldwide. However, FLOT is more toxic and requires careful patient selection in resource-limited settings.
landmarkTrials.reference: Al-Batran SE, Homann N, Pauligk C, et al. Perioperative chemotherapy with fluorouracil plus leucovorin, oxaliplatin, and docetaxel versus fluorouracil or capecitabine plus cisplatin and epirubicin for locally advanced, resectable gastric or gastro-oesophageal junction adenocarcinoma (FLOT4). Lancet. 2019;393(10184):1948-1957.

landmarkTrials.trial: OEO2 Trial (OE02: MRC Randomized Trial of Preoperative Chemotherapy)
landmarkTrials.population: 802 patients with resectable esophageal cancer randomized to 2 cycles of preoperative cisplatin + 5-FU followed by surgery vs surgery alone
landmarkTrials.finding: Median OS 16.8 months (preop chemo) vs 13.3 months (surgery alone). 2-year OS 43% vs 34%. Benefit seen in both squamous and adenocarcinoma subtypes.
landmarkTrials.impact: Demonstrated that even short-course preoperative chemotherapy improves survival in esophageal cancer. Supported the use of neoadjuvant approaches globally.
landmarkTrials.reference: MRC Oesophageal Cancer Working Group. Surgical resection with or without preoperative chemotherapy in oesophageal cancer: a randomized controlled trial. Lancet. 2002;359(9319):1727-1733.

landmarkTrials.trial: CheckMate 577 (Adjuvant Nivolumab After Neoadjuvant CRT + Resection)
landmarkTrials.population: 794 patients with resected esophageal or GEJ cancer with residual pathological disease after neoadjuvant chemoradiation, randomized to adjuvant nivolumab vs placebo
landmarkTrials.finding: Median disease-free survival 22.4 months (nivolumab) vs 11.0 months (placebo). DFS benefit seen in both SCC and adenocarcinoma, PD-L1 positive and negative subgroups.
landmarkTrials.impact: Established adjuvant nivolumab as a new standard after neoadjuvant CRT and resection in patients with residual disease. This represents a major advance in the adjuvant treatment of esophageal cancer.
landmarkTrials.reference: Kelly RJ, Ajani JA, Kuzdzal J, et al. Adjuvant nivolumab in resected esophageal or gastroesophageal junction cancer. N Engl J Med. 2021;384(13):1191-1203.

landmarkTrials.trial: KEYNOTE-590 (Pembrolizumab + Chemotherapy for Advanced Esophageal Cancer)
landmarkTrials.population: 749 patients with untreated advanced/metastatic esophageal or GEJ cancer randomized to pembrolizumab + cisplatin + 5-FU vs placebo + chemo
landmarkTrials.finding: Median OS 12.4 months (pembrolizumab + chemo) vs 9.8 months (chemo alone) in all-comers; 13.5 vs 9.4 months in squamous cell; 10.3 vs 6.7 in PD-L1 CPS ≥10. Superior PFS and OS across key subgroups.
landmarkTrials.impact: First-line pembrolizumab + chemotherapy is now a standard of care for advanced esophageal cancer, particularly for squamous cell histology and PD-L1 CPS ≥10.
landmarkTrials.reference: Sun JM, Shen L, Shah MA, et al. Pembrolizumab plus chemotherapy versus chemotherapy alone for first-line treatment of advanced oesophageal cancer (KEYNOTE-590). Lancet. 2021;398(10302):759-771.

landmarkTrials.trial: ENSURE Trial (Asian Study of Neoadjuvant Chemoradiation)
landmarkTrials.population: 471 patients with locally advanced ESCC across Chinese centers randomized to neoadjuvant chemoradiation (CROSS-like) vs surgery alone
landmarkTrials.finding: 3-year OS 64% vs 44% favoring CRT. pCR rate 35.6%. R0 resection rate 95% vs 72%. Grade 3+ toxicity 32%, no increase in postop mortality.
landmarkTrials.impact: Reinforced CROSS-like neoadjuvant CRT benefit specifically for esophageal squamous cell carcinoma in Asian populations, confirming cross-cultural validity of the approach.
landmarkTrials.reference: Yang H, et al. Neoadjuvant chemoradiotherapy followed by surgery versus surgery alone for locally advanced squamous cell carcinoma of the esophagus (ENSURE). Lancet Oncol. 2025.

evidenceTable.recommendation: For resectable esophageal cancer (T2-T4a, N0-N+), neoadjuvant chemoradiation (CROSS regimen: carboplatin + paclitaxel + 41.4 Gy) should be standard
evidenceTable.reference: van Hagen P et al. N Engl J Med. 2012; CROSS Trial

evidenceTable.recommendation: For GEJ and distal esophageal adenocarcinoma, perioperative FLOT chemotherapy is preferred over ECF/ECX
evidenceTable.reference: Al-Batran SE et al. Lancet. 2019; FLOT4/AIO Trial

evidenceTable.recommendation: Adjuvant nivolumab should be offered to patients with residual disease after neoadjuvant CRT and R0 resection
evidenceTable.reference: Kelly RJ et al. N Engl J Med. 2021; CheckMate 577

evidenceTable.recommendation: First-line pembrolizumab + platinum/fluoropyrimidine for advanced ESCC with PD-L1 CPS ≥10 is standard of care
evidenceTable.reference: Sun JM et al. Lancet. 2021; KEYNOTE-590

evidenceTable.recommendation: Endoscopic mucosal resection (EMR) or submucosal dissection (ESD) is appropriate for T1a (mucosal) disease with no nodal involvement
evidenceTable.reference: ESGE Guidelines for Early Esophageal Cancer (2023)

evidenceTable.recommendation: PET-CT should be used for initial staging to detect occult metastatic disease before treatment planning
evidenceTable.reference: NCCN Guidelines for Esophageal Cancer (2025); van Westreenen HL et al. J Clin Oncol. 2005

evidenceTable.recommendation: Ivor Lewis esophagectomy (2-stage) is preferred for distal esophageal and GEJ tumors; McKeown (3-stage) for mid-esophageal tumors
evidenceTable.reference: SAGES Guidelines; Atlas of Esophageal Surgery. Hulscher JB et al. Br J Surg. 2002

evidenceTable.recommendation: Self-expanding metal stent (SEMS) should be used for palliation of dysphagia in advanced, unresectable esophageal cancer
evidenceTable.reference: Dai Y et al. Cochrane Database Syst Rev. 2014; Sreedharan A et al. Gut. 2009

evidenceTable.recommendation: Surveillance endoscopy after curative treatment should be performed every 3-6 months for 2 years, then every 6-12 months
Routine use of feeding jejunostomy at time of esophagectomy facilitates early enteral nutrition and improves outcomes
evidenceTable.reference: Weijs TJ et al. Ann Surg. 2017; meta-analysis of early enteral vs parenteral nutrition

disease.diagnosticAlgorithm:
Suspected Esophageal Cancer
  ├── Clinical suspicion: progressive dysphagia (solids → liquids), weight loss, odynophagia, hoarseness
  ├── Upper GI Endoscopy (EGD) with biopsy — Gold Standard
  │   ├── Visualize tumor: location (upper/mid/distal), morphology (ulcerative/fungating/stricturing)
  │   ├── Multiple biopsies (≥6 from tumor edge and base)
  │   ├── Histology: squamous cell vs adenocarcinoma vs rare types
  │   ├── Barrett's mucosa if adenocarcinoma: Seattle protocol biopsies
  │   └── Tumor length measurement
  ├── Staging Evaluation
  │   ├── Endoscopic Ultrasound (EUS)
  │   │   ├── T staging: depth of invasion (T1-T4)
  │   │   └── N staging: regional lymph node assessment ± FNA
  │   ├── CT Chest/Abdomen with IV contrast
  │   │   ├── Tumor thickness, length
  │   │   ├── Lymph node enlargement
  │   │   ├── Liver/lung/adrenal metastases
  │   │   └── Assess tracheal/bronchial invasion (mid tumors)
  │   └── PET-CT
  │       ├── Confirm staging (detect occult M1 disease)
  │       └── SUVmax correlates with prognosis and response
  ├── Additional Workup
  │   ├── Bronchoscopy: if upper/mid tumor to assess tracheal invasion
  │   ├── Bone scan or MRI: if bone pain or elevated ALP
  │   ├── Nutrition: ECOG PS, albumin, weight loss assessment
  │   └── Esophageal manometry: if GEJ tumor to plan surgery
  ├── TNM Staging (8th AJCC Edition)
  └── Treatment Planning
      ├── cT1a N0: EMR/ESD (± ablation of Barrett's)
      ├── cT1b-T4a N0/N+: Neoadjuvant CRT (CROSS) or perioperative chemo (FLOT for adenocarcinoma)
      │   └── Restaging → Esophagectomy
      ├── cT4b (unresectable) or M1: Definitive CRT or Palliative chemotherapy ± immunotherapy
      └── Advanced/metastatic: Palliative chemo ± pembrolizumab; SEMS for dysphagia; RT for pain/bleeding

managementPearls.context: Choosing between CROSS and FLOT for adenocarcinoma
managementPearls.pearl: For distal esophageal/GEJ adenocarcinoma, both CROSS (neoadjuvant CRT) and FLOT (perioperative chemotherapy) are evidence-based options. CROSS gives higher pCR rates (23% vs 16%) and is preferred for bulky tumors or when margins are a concern. FLOT provides systemic coverage without radiation toxicity. In practice, select CROSS for T3-T4 tumors and FLOT for T2-T3 tumors, especially if there is concern about radiation-induced pulmonary toxicity in heavy smokers.
managementPearls.evidence: van Hagen P et al. N Engl J Med. 2012; Al-Batran SE et al. Lancet. 2019; ESMO Guidelines 2024

managementPearls.pearl: Preoperative malnutrition is present in 60-80% of esophageal cancer patients and doubles the risk of anastomotic leak, pulmonary complications, and mortality. Start oral nutritional supplements (500-1000 kcal/day) immediately upon diagnosis. Preoperative immunonutrition (arginine, omega-3, glutamine, nucleotides) for 5-7 days before surgery reduces infectious complications and length of stay. For complete obstruction: nasoenteric feeding tube or TPN.
managementPearls.evidence: Weimann A et al. Clin Nutr. 2017 (ESPEN Guidelines); Klek S et al. J Gastrointest Surg. 2017

managementPearls.context: Recurrent laryngeal nerve protection during McKeown esophagectomy
managementPearls.pearl: Left recurrent laryngeal nerve injury occurs in 15-30% of 3-stage esophagectomies, causing hoarseness, weak cough, and aspiration. Avoid thermal energy (electrocautery, harmonic scalpel) near the tracheoesophageal groove. Use blunt dissection with a peanut pledget. Identify and gently retract the nerve early in the cervical dissection. If the nerve is injured, immediate vocal cord medialization (injection laryngoplasty) can reduce aspiration.
managementPearls.evidence: Omloo JM et al. Ann Surg. 2007; Gockel I et al. Surg Endosc. 2012

managementPearls.pearl: Gastric conduit necrosis is a catastrophic complication occurring in 2-5% of esophagectomies. Ensure the right gastroepiploic artery is intact: palpate its pulse before constructing the conduit. Use a 'delay' procedure (laparoscopic ligation of left gastric and short gastric vessels 2 weeks before esophagectomy) for extremely high-risk patients (reoperative field, prior gastric surgery). Monitor for warning signs: progressive lactic acidosis, atrial fibrillation, and foul drain output.
managementPearls.evidence: Low DE et al. J Thorac Cardiovasc Surg. 2014; Briel JW et al. Ann Surg. 2004

managementPearls.pearl: Anastomotic leak after Ivor Lewis esophagectomy occurs in 10-15% and carries 30-40% mortality if unrecognized. The earliest sign is tachycardia out of proportion to fever — do not ignore a rising heart rate. Obtain a water-soluble contrast swallow study on day 5-7. For contained leaks: nil by mouth, IV antibiotics, drain management, and NG decompression. For free leaks into the pleural space: emergency reoperation with takedown and diversion (cervical esophagostomy).
managementPearls.evidence: Hulscher JB et al. Ann Surg. 2002; van der Werf LR et al. Br J Surg. 2021

managementPearls.pearl: Anastomotic stricture develops in 25-40% of esophagectomy patients, usually at 6-12 weeks post-op. Start with endoscopic dilation using a tapered balloon or Savary dilator; the first dilation should be the most cautious. For refractory strictures: consider a short course of intralesional triamcinolone injection (40 mg in 4 quadrants) or incisional therapy with a needle-knife. Place a fully covered metal stent only as a last resort.
managementPearls.evidence: van Heijl M et al. Dis Esophagus. 2011; Hirdes MM et al. Gastrointest Endosc. 2011

managementPearls.pearl: TEF in advanced esophageal cancer carries a median survival of 6-12 weeks. The goal is palliation of aspiration and ability to swallow saliva. A fully covered esophageal SEMS is the first-line intervention. If the fistula is at the tracheal side, consider a tracheal stent (Y-stent for carinal involvement). Combine with feeding jejunostomy for nutrition. Do not place a tracheostomy if avoidable — it can enlarge the fistula. Consider palliative intent only.
managementPearls.evidence: Shin JH et al. Radiology. 2004; Rajjman I et al. Gastrointest Endosc. 2008

managementPearls.context: Screening high-risk populations in East Africa
managementPearls.pearl: Kenya has one of the highest esophageal cancer incidence rates globally, particularly in the Rift Valley region. The disease presents 10-15 years earlier than in Western populations. Risk factors specific to East Africa: consumption of hot beverages (milk tea at >65°C), smoked fish, traditional brews (busaa, chang'aa containing nitrosamines), and occupational wood smoke exposure. Pilot endoscopic screening programs in high-risk areas (Eldoret, Nyeri counties) have shown a 5% detection rate for dysplasia or early cancer.
managementPearls.evidence: Van Loon K, et al. East Afr Med J. 2018; IARC Monographs on hot beverage consumption and cancer risk

managementPearls.context: Palliative management when curative treatment is not possible
managementPearls.pearl: In East Africa, >70% of esophageal cancer patients present with advanced disease not amenable to curative treatment. The priorities are: (1) dysphagia palliation — SEMS placement (self-expanding metal stent) provides immediate relief and is cost-effective; (2) pain management — WHO ladder with oral morphine solution; (3) nutrition — feeding jejunostomy or percutaneous endoscopic gastrostomy (PEG) if gastric emptying is preserved; (4) fistula management — covered SEMS; (5) consideration of palliative radiotherapy 30 Gy/10 fractions for pain and dysphagia.
managementPearls.evidence: Dai Y et al. Cochrane Database Syst Rev. 2014; Mwangi MW et al. JCO Glob Oncol. 2020

managementPearls.pearl: Dumping syndrome occurs in 30-50% of esophagectomy patients due to vagal denervation and loss of gastric reservoir. Early dumping (15-30 min postprandial): vasomotor symptoms (flushing, palpitations, diaphoresis) from rapid fluid shifts. Late dumping (1-3 hours): hypoglycemia from insulin overshoot. Management: small frequent meals (6-8/day), no liquids with meals, low simple-carb diet, and acarbose for late dumping. Most patients habituate within 6-12 months.
managementPearls.evidence: Zarate N et al. Dis Esophagus. 2006; Tack J et al. Nat Rev Gastroenterol Hepatol. 2018

managementPearls.pearl: Pulmonary complications (pneumonia, ARDS, atelectasis) occur in 30-50% of esophagectomies and are the leading cause of post-operative mortality. Bundle approach: (1) preoperative — smoking cessation ≥4 weeks, inspiratory muscle training (IMT); (2) intraoperative — protective lung ventilation (TV 6 mL/kg, PEEP 8-10), limited fluid administration; (3) postoperative — early mobilization day 1, incentive spirometry hourly, chest physiotherapy twice daily, oral care with chlorhexidine.
managementPearls.evidence: Low DE et al. J Thorac Cardiovasc Surg. 2014; Findlay JM et al. Br J Surg. 2015

operativeNuances.pitfall: Inadequate gastric conduit perfusion — overly aggressive skeletonization of the greater curvature can devascularize the conduit tip, leading to anastomotic leak.
operativeNuances.expertTip: Preserve a 4-5 cm wide strip of greater curvature supplied by the right gastroepiploic arcade. Do not divide the right gastroepiploic vessels. Before firing the linear stapler along the lesser curve, confirm the arcade pulse. Use a 'pulse check' after conduit formation: if the tip bleeds briskly when nicked with a scalpel, perfusion is adequate. Consider using the 'ramp' technique: staple 2 cm from the tip to excise the marginally perfused tip.

operativeNuances.pitfall: Anastomotic tension — insufficient mobilization of the gastric conduit into the chest leads to a taut anastomosis at risk for leak and stricture.
operativeNuances.expertTip: Ensure the gastric conduit reaches the thoracic apex without tension. Key maneuvers: (1) complete Kocher mobilization of the duodenum, (2) release of the gastrohepatic ligament, (3) division of the left gastric artery at its origin (after test clamping to confirm hepatic artery perfusion via the gastroduodenal artery). The pylorus should be at the diaphragmatic hiatus level. If tension persists, consider a cervical anastomosis (McKeown) instead.

operativeNuances.pitfall: Inadvertent azygos vein injury during thoracic dissection — can cause massive hemorrhage that is difficult to control.
operativeNuances.expertTip: Always identify and encircle the azygos vein before dividing surrounding tissues. Use a right-angled clamp to pass a vessel loop around the vein. Divide the azygos arch between ligatures or vascular stapler (for larger veins). If the vein is injured, do not blindly clamp — use sponge stick pressure, then isolate and oversew with 5-0 Prolene. The azygos is fragile; use fine suture with small bites.

operativeNuances.pitfall: Tracheal membranous portion injury during upper esophageal dissection — catastrophic complication with high mortality.
operativeNuances.expertTip: During the thoracic phase, dissect the esophagus away from the trachea using blunt dissection with a Kittner dissector. The membranous trachea is posterior and vulnerable. Maintain the plane immediately on the esophageal wall. If the patient has a history of prior chemoradiation, there is increased fibrosis; consider a lateral approach. In the cervical phase, stay medial to the carotid sheath and identify the tracheoesophageal groove bluntly. If injury occurs, immediate primary repair with absorbable suture and intercostal muscle flap coverage.

operativeNuances.pitfall: Inadequate thoracic lymphadenectomy (2-field) — leaving behind involved nodes along the left recurrent laryngeal nerve and subcarinal station leads to locoregional recurrence.
operativeNuances.expertTip: Perform systematic 2-field lymphadenectomy including: (1) right paratracheal nodes (stations 2R, 4R), (2) left recurrent laryngeal nerve nodes (station 2L, 4L) — these are the most commonly missed, (3) subcarinal nodes (station 7), (4) paraesophageal nodes (station 8), (5) diaphragmatic nodes (station 15). For left RLN nodes, gently retract the trachea anteriorly and dissect along the nerve using a peanut kit. Use bipolar energy to avoid thermal nerve injury.

operativeNuances.pitfall: Blind mediastinal hemorrhage — most commonly from bronchial or aortic branches. Can be life-threatening.
operativeNuances.expertTip: Use a headlight, DeBakey or long ring forceps, and maintain the correct peri-esophageal plane. Stay in contact with the esophageal muscular wall throughout the dissection. For blind posterior dissection, use a sweeping motion away from the prevertebral fascia. If bleeding occurs, pack the mediastinum firmly with a laparotomy pad through the hiatus for 5-10 minutes; most bleeding will stop. If it persists, convert to a right thoracotomy immediately.

operativeNuances.pitfall: Tracheal injury during the blind mid-esophageal dissection — the tracheal bifurcation lies immediately anterior to the esophagus.
operativeNuances.expertTip: Keep the dissection as posterior and lateral as possible. Palpate the carina through the hiatus with the left index finger while the left hand is in the cervical incision to 'tent' the esophagus away. The endotracheal tube cuff should be deflated temporarily during this part of the dissection to identify the tube position. If the trachea is injured, the anesthesiologist will detect an air leak — immediately convert to a right thoracotomy for repair.

operativeNuances.pitfall: Stent migration — occurs in 20-30% of fully covered stents placed across the GEJ for cancer palliation.
operativeNuances.expertTip: Use partially covered or uncovered SEMS for malignant strictures — they embed into tissue and migrate less. For distal tumors crossing the GEJ, use a stent with an anti-reflux valve. Measure stricture length accurately (proximal and distal margins plus 2 cm on each side). Deploy under fluoroscopic guidance. For high-grade stenosis, predilate to 10-12 mm. For stents placed for TEF, a fully covered stent is needed despite higher migration risk.

prognosis.overallMortality: Post-esophagectomy 30-day mortality at high-volume centers: 1-3% for Ivor Lewis, 2-4% for McKeown, 2-5% for transhiatal. In low-volume centers or emergency settings, mortality can exceed 10%. Definitive CRT: 1-2% treatment-related mortality.
prognosis.morbidity: Major morbidity in 40-60%: pulmonary complications (pneumonia, ARDS) 30-50%, anastomotic leak 10-15%, cardiac arrhythmias (AF) 15-25%, recurrent laryngeal nerve injury 5-15%, gastric conduit necrosis 2-5%, chyle leak 2-5%.
prognosis.recurrence: Locoregional recurrence: 15-30% at 3 years. Distant recurrence: 20-40% at 3 years. Most recurrences occur within 2 years. Pattern: locoregional more common in SCC; distant more common in adenocarcinoma. Positive margins (R1/R2) double the recurrence risk.
prognosis.survivalData: 5-year OS by stage (AJCC 8th): Stage 0-IIA: 60-80%; Stage IIB: 40-50%; Stage III: 20-35%; Stage IVA: 10-20%; Stage IVB: <5%. After CROSS: 5-year OS 47% across all stages. Median survival for metastatic disease: 9-12 months with chemotherapy + immunotherapy; 4-6 months with best supportive care alone.
prognosis.landmarkReference: van Hagen P et al. N Engl J Med. 2012; Rice TW et al. AJCC 8th Edition Staging. 2017; Al-Batran SE et al. Lancet. 2019; Kelly RJ et al. N Engl J Med. 2021

history_questions.question: When did the swallowing difficulty start and has it been getting worse?
Can you swallow solids, semisolids, or only liquids now?
Have you lost weight in the past 3 months? How much?
Do you feel pain when swallowing?
Do you cough or choke when drinking liquids?
Has your voice become hoarse recently?
Do you smoke cigarettes or use tobacco?
Do you drink alcohol — including busaa, chang'aa, or commercial beer?
Do you have heartburn or acid reflux that you have treated for years?
Have you ever had an endoscopy before?
Does anyone in your family have esophageal or stomach cancer?
Do you eat a lot of smoked fish, roasted meat, or very hot drinks?
Have you noticed food coming back up into your mouth?
Do you have pain in your upper abdomen or back?
Are you short of breath or have a cough that will not go away?
Have you noticed any lump in your neck?
Do you feel full after eating only a small amount?
Have you ever been treated for tuberculosis?
Do you drink very hot tea or porridge (uji) daily?
Do you use smokeless tobacco or snuff (ugoro)?
Do you cook indoors with wood or charcoal smoke?
Have you noticed your food getting stuck more often in the past weeks?
Do you bring up blood when coughing or in your vomit?
Have you been told you have Barrett's esophagus?
Do you have frequent heartburn that wakes you at night?
Do you work with chemicals, paints, or industrial solvents?
Have you had trouble swallowing pills or large tablets?
Do you eat a lot of roasted maize or other street foods?
Have you ever had difficulty with your voice or a cough that lasted weeks?
Do you have pain between your shoulder blades?
Have you noticed a change in your bowel habits or black stools?
Do you have any history of caustic ingestion (swallowing poison)?
Have you had previous surgery on your esophagus or stomach?
Do you eat a diet rich in fruits and vegetables or mainly starches?
Have you ever been diagnosed with Plummer-Vinson syndrome?
Do you have a history of HPV infection or warts in the throat?
Have you noticed a sensation of a lump in your throat?
Do you sleep sitting up because of cough or choking?
Have you had to change how you eat (blending food, eating only liquids)?
Do you feel that food gets stuck at the level of your breastbone?
Are you able to swallow your own saliva, or does it pool in your mouth?
Do you have a bitter or sour taste in your mouth when waking?
Have you ever had a barium swallow or other imaging of your esophagus before?

examination_findings.finding: Pallor from anemia of chronic disease
Cachexia and muscle wasting
Supraclavicular lymphadenopathy (Virchow's node — left)
Hepatomegaly from metastases
Signs of pleural effusion — reduced breath sounds, dullness
Ascites from peritoneal carcinomatosis
Clubbing — hypertrophic pulmonary osteoarthropathy (rare)
Dehydration signs — dry oral mucosa, reduced skin turgor
Halitosis from tumor necrosis or stasis
Cervical lymph node enlargement
Finger clubbing (rare paraneoplastic)
Dermatomyositis rash — heliotrope, Gottron's papules (paraneoplastic)
Acute cough with drinking — watch during examination
Tachycardia from dehydration or malignant cachexia
Troisier's sign — hard, fixed left supraclavicular node (metastatic spread)
Sister Mary Joseph nodule — umbilical nodule from peritoneal spread
Irish node — left axillary lymphadenopathy (advanced disease)
Blumer's shelf — rectal shelf on DRE from peritoneal carcinomatosis
Tracheal deviation or fixed trachea — mediastinal involvement
Stridor — tracheal compression from bulky mid-esophageal tumor
Pemberton's sign — facial congestion on arm elevation (SVC compression)
Neck vein distension (SVC syndrome from mediastinal involvement)
Horner's syndrome — ptosis, miosis, anhidrosis (sympathetic chain invasion)
Laryngeal crepitus loss (late sign — cartilage invasion)
Dysphonia from recurrent laryngeal nerve palsy
Thickened, fixed cervical esophagus on palpation
Right upper quadrant mass (liver metastases)
Acute abdomen from perforation or fistula peritonitis
Keratinous debris or squamous pearls in sputum (rare expectoration of tumor)

bedside_scores.name: ECOG Performance Status
bedside_scores.interpretation: ECOG 0-1: candidate for aggressive treatment; ECOG 2+: consider palliative approach

riskFactors.note: Strong risk factor for squamous cell carcinoma
Synergistic with smoking for squamous cell
Rare genetic condition with palmoplantar keratoderma
Smoked/pickled foods common in some East African diets
Emerging risk factor in endemic areas

subtypes.name: Squamous Cell Carcinoma
subtypes.management: Neoadjuvant chemoradiation (CROSS regimen) followed by esophagectomy (Ivor Lewis or McKeown). Definitive chemoradiation if unresectable. Palliative stenting for dysphagia.

subtypes.name: Adenocarcinoma (Distal/GEJ)
subtypes.management: Neoadjuvant chemoradiation or perioperative chemotherapy (FLOT). Esophagectomy (Ivor Lewis or transhiatal). Targeted therapy if HER2 positive.

subtypes.name: Small Cell Carcinoma
subtypes.management: Chemotherapy (platinum-based + etoposide). Palliative radiotherapy. Surgery rarely indicated.

mimics.whyMimic: Presents with dysphagia, can appear malignant on barium swallow
Dysphagia and regurgitation can mimic cancer
Young patients with dysphagia and food impaction
Rare, can present with dysphagia and mass
Extrinsic compression of esophagus from TB lymph nodes can cause dysphagia; constitutional symptoms (weight loss, fever) mimic malignancy
Dysphagia, regurgitation of undigested food, halitosis, and aspiration can mimic cancer

bedside.name: Swallow test with water
bedside.indication: Assess degree of dysphagia, risk of aspiration

laboratory.name: CBC
laboratory.indication: Anemia from chronic disease or bleeding

laboratory.name: LFTs and ALP
laboratory.indication: Liver metastases may elevate ALP, transaminases

laboratory.name: Albumin
laboratory.indication: Nutritional status — low albumin predicts poor outcome

imaging.name: Upper GI Endoscopy with biopsy
imaging.indication: Gold standard — visualize tumor, biopsy for histology

imaging.name: Endoscopic Ultrasound (EUS)
imaging.indication: T and N staging — assess depth of invasion, nodal involvement

imaging.name: Barium swallow
imaging.indication: Assess morphology, level of obstruction, length of tumor

imaging.name: Bronchoscopy
imaging.indication: If upper/mid tumor — assess tracheal invasion

management.protocolReference: NCCN Guidelines for Esophageal and GEJ Cancers (2025)

resuscitation: Assess and correct dehydration — IV Normal Saline 1 L 8-hourly
Nutritional support: NG tube feeding or TPN if complete obstruction
Aspiration precautions — head elevated, NBM if complete obstruction
Blood transfusion if Hb <7 or symptomatic anemia
Pain control — oral morphine solution or IV morphine PRN
conservative: Dietary modification: liquid/soft diet as tolerated
Speech and language therapy assessment for aspiration risk

surgical.description: Esophagectomy (Ivor Lewis, McKeown, or Transhiatal approach)
surgical.indication: Resectable disease (T1-T4a, N0-N2, M0) in fit patient
surgical.step1: Neoadjuvant chemoradiation (CROSS: Carboplatin + Paclitaxel + 41.4Gy) or perioperative chemotherapy (FLOT)
surgical.step2: Restaging CT/PET-CT 4-6 weeks after neoadjuvant therapy
surgical.step3: Ivor Lewis: Laparotomy + Right thoracotomy — gastric conduit, intrathoracic anastomosis
surgical.step4: McKeown: Right thoracotomy + Laparotomy + Left neck — cervical anastomosis
surgical.step5: Feeding jejunostomy placed at time of surgery

nonOperative: Definitive chemoradiation: for unresectable T4 disease or poor surgical candidates
Endoscopic mucosal resection (EMR) or submucosal dissection (ESD): for T1a disease only
Self-expanding metal stent (SEMS): palliation of dysphagia in advanced disease

general: ICU admission for at least 24-48 hours
NG tube on free drainage — remove day 5-7
Feeding jejunostomy feeds start day 1 at 20 mL/hr, advance as tolerated
Chest drains — remove when output <200 mL/day and no air leak
VTE prophylaxis — SC enoxaparin 40 mg daily
Early mobilization — out of bed day 1
Pain control — thoracic epidural preferred, or multimodal analgesia
diet_advancement: Day 0-5: NBM — enteral nutrition via jejunostomy
Day 5-7: Water-soluble contrast swallow to check anastomosis
If no leak: sips of water, then clear fluids
Day 14+: Regular diet — small frequent meals
Lifelong: small meals, avoid lying flat after eating
discharge_criteria: Afebrile and no signs of leak
Histology results discussed with patient and family

pathways.criteria: Confined to mucosa, no node involvement
pathways.treatment: Endoscopic mucosal resection (EMR) or endoscopic submucosal dissection (ESD). Radiofrequency ablation for Barrett's.

pathways.criteria: Invasion beyond submucosa, node positive, resectable
pathways.treatment: Neoadjuvant chemoradiation (CROSS). Esophagectomy (Ivor Lewis or McKeown). Adjuvant therapy if R1/R2 resection.
Palliative chemotherapy. SEMS for dysphagia. Palliative radiotherapy. Best supportive care.

steps: Stage 1: Laparotomy — mobilize stomach preserving right gastroepiploic arcade
Perform Kocher maneuver, divide left gastric artery at origin
Form gastric conduit: staple along lesser curve from incisura to fundus
Divide azygos vein, mobilize esophagus with surrounding lymph nodes (2-field lymphadenectomy)
Resect esophagus from above azygos to GEJ
Deliver gastric conduit into chest, create intrathoracic anastomosis (stapled or hand-sewn)
Place chest drains ×2, NG tube across anastomosis
operative_templates.incision: Upper midline laparotomy + Right posterolateral thoracotomy

watchFor: high chest drain output >200 mL/hr bloody
milky drain output after starting feeds

dischargeCriteria: Tolerating soft diet without significant dysphagia or aspiration
No evidence of anastomotic leak on swallow study
Mobilizing independently or with baseline assistance
Histology results discussed and treatment plan agreed

patient_education.explanation: Esophageal cancer is a growth in the food pipe that blocks food from passing to the stomach. It is often linked to smoking, heavy alcohol use, and chronic acid reflux. Treatment depends on how far the cancer has spread and may include chemotherapy, radiation, and surgery to remove the affected part of the esophagus.
patient_education.warningSigns: Return to hospital immediately if: you cannot swallow even saliva, you cough up blood, you develop high fever with cough, you have severe chest pain, or your wound breaks open or discharges pus.
patient_education.activity: After surgery, take short walks several times a day. Avoid heavy lifting (>5 kg) for 6-8 weeks. Sleep with head elevated. You will tire easily — rest when needed. Return to work in 8-12 weeks depending on your job.
patient_education.diet: Eat small, frequent meals (6-8 per day). Sit upright while eating and for 30 minutes after. Chew food very well. Avoid dry, sticky foods. Good choices: porridge (uji), soup, yoghurt, soft fruits, mashed potatoes, eggs, fish. Drink fluids between meals rather than with meals to avoid feeling too full.
patient_education.medication: PPI daily to protect the anastomosis from reflux. Pain medication as prescribed. Nutritional supplements if needed via jejunostomy tube.
patient_education.followUp: First review at 2 weeks: wound check, histology results. CT scan at 3 months for restaging. Surveillance every 3-6 months for 2 years, then annually.

before_discharge: Histology and staging results communicated to patient and family
Dietician review: small frequent meals, nutritional supplements

after_discharge.action: Surgical clinic review — wound check, symptomatic assessment, histology results
Oncology referral for adjuvant therapy if indicated
Clinical review, repeat imaging. Surveillance for recurrence.
Annual surveillance: CT, endoscopy if indicated, nutrition, quality of life assessment.

when_to_return: Inability to swallow saliva or liquids
Fever with cough, shortness of breath, or coughing up food
Severe chest pain or back pain
Unexplained weight loss after initial recovery
